title: Election 2024 live updates: Harris picks Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz as her running mate, AP sources say
summary:
Before selecting Tim Walz as her running mate, Vice President Kamala Harris' campaign purchased the domain name ClintonKaine.com from a domain name investor who had previously sold it to the Trump campaign. The investor, Jeremy Green, had originally offered the domain to Clinton's campaign but was rejected. Green then sold it to the Trump campaign, which used it to push anti-Clinton news.

After Harris chose Walz as her running mate, Green created a blank website with Walz's name on it, inspired by a tweet referring to Harris as a "brat." He has expressed interest in selling the site to the Harris campaign despite his previous experience with the Trump campaign.

Green believes that the Harris campaign should purchase the domain name to prevent others from using it to spread misinformation or negative content. He views the sale as a business transaction and is not concerned about potential backlash for selling to the campaign that he supports.

title: Fast-moving San Bernardino wildfire torches hillside community, forcing evacuations
summary:
A fast-moving wildfire ravaged a hillside community in San Bernardino, California, on Monday, burning several homes and forcing evacuations. Over 200 firefighters fought the blaze, which grew to 54 acres before its spread was halted.

Arson investigators are investigating the cause of the fire, while one person detained was later released. Evacuation orders remain in effect for residents south of Ridge Line Drive and north of Edgehill Road.

Amid the devastation, videos emerged of residents fleeing properties amidst smoke and flames. One video captured a man carrying a turkey uphill as the fire raged behind him. With temperatures reaching 100 degrees Fahrenheit, the National Weather Service has issued an excessive heat warning for the area until Tuesday.

title: Bangladesh frees Hasina rival Khaleda Zia from house arrest
summary:
Bangladesh has released Begum Khaleda Zia, a former prime minister and leader of the opposition, from house arrest. Zia, 78, had been convicted of corruption and sentenced to 17 years in prison in 2018. Her release came after the resignation of Prime Minister Sheikh Hasina and calls from a student-led movement for an interim government.

Zia was a political rival of Hasina, and her terms as prime minister were marked by corruption allegations. Her release was part of a deal that saw her two sons relocate to the UK.

Meanwhile, the Bangladesh police association has apologized for actions against protesters and demanded the release of its jailed officers. Human Rights Watch has accused security forces of extrajudicial killings and enforced disappearances during Hasina's rule, which the government denies. Families of political prisoners, including opposition activist Ahmad Bin Quasem, are awaiting news of their loved ones' release.

title: Tropical Storm Debby takes aim at Savannah and Charleston
summary:
Tropical Storm Debby poses a significant flooding threat to historic southern cities, including Savannah and Charleston. The storm has already claimed at least five lives in Florida, including a child killed by a fallen tree and a driver who crashed into a canal.

Debby's slow pace is expected to lead to "catastrophic flooding" in parts of Georgia, South Carolina, and North Carolina, with rainfall reaching 20 inches in some areas. The storm is also bringing strong winds and high waves, leading to downed power lines and disruptions in the region.

As of Tuesday morning, Debby's center was positioned near Savannah and Charleston, with the storm forecast to continue along the coast before moving inland near South Carolina on Thursday. Authorities are urging residents to take precautions and heed evacuation orders to avoid the potentially deadly flooding and other hazards associated with the storm.

title: Lebanon's Hezbollah launches drone attacks on Israel, says more to come
A Virginia man has been arrested for allegedly threatening to kill Kamala Harris
Former Trump attorney Jenna Ellis to cooperate in fake electors case
summary:
Former Trump attorney Jenna Ellis has agreed to cooperate with prosecutors in the investigation into the fake electors scheme. Ellis met with the Fulton County District Attorney's office last month, but the details of her cooperation have not been made public.

The fake electors scheme was an attempt by Trump supporters to overturn the results of the 2020 presidential election. In several states, including Georgia, individuals were selected to serve as electors and cast electoral votes for Trump, even though he had lost the popular vote.

Ellis is one of several Trump associates who have been subpoenaed or questioned by prosecutors in connection with the fake electors scheme. Her cooperation could provide valuable information about the Trump campaign's involvement in the plot.

title: Everything was going Kamala Harris’ way. Then came the market sell-off.
summary:
Vice President Kamala Harris had previously enjoyed a surge in popularity, with her approval ratings rising. However, a recent sell-off in the stock market has caused her momentum to stall. The decline in the market has made voters more concerned about the economy, and Harris has struggled to address their fears effectively.

This has been a challenge for the Biden administration as they attempt to recover from the COVID-19 pandemic and its economic impact. The drop in the market has made some voters wary of the administration's economic policies. As a result, Harris' approval ratings have suffered, and she is now facing more pressure to deliver results.

Harris is not the only politician who has been affected by the market sell-off. President Biden's approval ratings have also declined, and the Democratic Party as a whole is facing headwinds heading into the 2022 midterm elections. The economic downturn has made it more difficult for the Democrats to make their case to voters, and the Republicans are hoping to capitalize on this.

title: Bloomberg gives $600 million to four Black medical schools’ endowments
summary:
Michael Bloomberg's organization, Bloomberg Philanthropies, is donating $600 million to the endowments of four historically Black medical schools: Howard University College of Medicine, Meharry Medical College, Morehouse School of Medicine, and Charles Drew University of Medicine & Science. This generous donation will significantly increase the size of three of the schools' endowments. It aims to empower new generations of Black doctors and address health disparities faced by Black Americans.

The gift follows Bloomberg's $1 billion pledge to Johns Hopkins University to eliminate tuition for most medical students. Bloomberg Philanthropies' Greenwood Initiative, focused on reducing the racial wealth gap, has played a key role in supporting this latest donation to the medical schools. The endowment funds will allow the schools to enhance their programs, alleviate student debt, and increase their overall impact on the healthcare system.

Experts emphasize the importance of increasing Black representation among doctors as a crucial step in addressing long-standing health inequities. Currently, only 6% of U.S. physicians are Black despite Black Americans representing 13% of the population. The donation to these historically Black medical schools is seen as a major step towards addressing this disparity and creating a more equitable healthcare system.

title: Recreational Rollout: What to expect as recreational marijuana sales begin in Ohio
summary:
Ohio has finally legalized recreational marijuana sales, with licensed dispensaries across the state now allowed to sell cannabis products. After months of waiting and regulatory hurdles, Ohioans who are 21 or older can purchase recreational marijuana for adult use.

Before visiting a dispensary, customers must present a valid ID to prove their age. They are not permitted to touch or open any cannabis products on the premises until they have made a purchase.

Some dispensaries experienced long lines of eager customers ahead of opening, with some individuals waiting overnight to secure early access to the newly legalized products. Industry officials are urging patience as dispensaries adjust to the high volume of patrons and potential operational challenges.
